regions: Midwest: Chicago IL, Kansas City MO/KS, Omaha NE, Sioux City IA, St. Paul MN, Fort Worth TX
Upper Midwest: Waterloo IA, Austin MN, Sioux Falls SD

what_they_did.daily_work: Worked on the disassembly line in a slaughterhouse — the reverse of an assembly line. Each worker performed one repetitive task: stunning, sticking (bleeding), skinning, splitting, trimming, or packing. Speed was dictated by the chain that moved carcasses through the plant. Upton Sinclair described it in 1906 and conditions remained brutal for decades.
what_they_did.seasonal_cycle: Year-round employment, though hours fluctuated with livestock supply. Fall and early winter (October-January) were peak slaughter months when cattle and hogs came to market after fattening. Summer could bring shorter weeks.
tools_and_equipment: Stunning hammer or captive bolt gun (after 1920s)
what_they_did.typical_hours: 10-12 hours per day, 6 days a week before unionization. After UPWA contracts (1940s-1950s): 8-hour days, 5-day weeks with overtime pay.

economics.typical_arrangement: Paid hourly. Before unionization, wages were set unilaterally by the Big Four packers (Armour, Swift, Wilson, Cudahy). After the United Packinghouse Workers of America (UPWA) organized in the 1940s, wages rose significantly through collective bargaining.
economics.annual_income_range: 1900-1910: $6-$10 per week ($300-$520/year). 1920s: $25-$35 per week ($1,200-$1,800/year). Post-UPWA 1950s: $65-$80 per week ($3,400-$4,200/year). These were among the best-paying industrial jobs available to Black workers and immigrants.
economics.comparison: By the 1950s, a unionized meatpacking worker earned wages comparable to auto and steel workers, and far more than any agricultural work available in the South. This wage differential was a primary driver of the Great Migration.
economics.debt_cycle: Unlike agricultural debt traps, meatpacking workers received cash wages. However, early immigrant workers often owed debts to labor agents (padrones) who arranged their travel and initial housing.

hazards: Repetitive strain injuries from high-speed line work
Respiratory problems from ammonia refrigeration leaks
Burns from scalding water used in hog processing
working_conditions.living_conditions: Workers lived in neighborhoods adjacent to the stockyards — Back of the Yards in Chicago, the Bottoms in Kansas City. These areas suffered from the stench of the plants, poor sanitation, and overcrowded housing. Packingtown neighborhoods were sharply divided by ethnicity.
working_conditions.family_involvement: Women worked in packing plants, especially in trimming and sausage-making departments. Children were legally employed in plants before child labor laws; after 1916 reforms, teenagers still worked during summers.
working_conditions.autonomy: Very little before unionization — workers could be fired at will, were subject to speedups, and had no grievance process. The UPWA transformed conditions after the 1940s, establishing seniority rights, grievance procedures, and safety standards.

push_factors: Plant closures as packers moved from urban stockyard cities to rural plants (1960s-1980s)
Industry consolidation eliminated competition for labor
Deunionization as IBP and other new packers broke UPWA contracts
why_they_left.what_replaced_them: The old urban stockyard plants closed (Chicago Stockyards shut in 1971). The industry relocated to rural towns in Iowa, Nebraska, Kansas, and North Carolina, hiring immigrant labor at lower wages. The high-wage, unionized meatpacking job essentially ceased to exist.
why_they_left.narrative_hook: The packinghouse that drew {ancestor_name} north from {county} to {destination} offered something the cotton fields never could — a weekly paycheck, cash money, and the possibility that next year might be better than this one.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Hire dates, job classifications, wages, department assignments, sometimes home addresses and next of kin
records_generated.where_to_find: Wisconsin Historical Society (holds major Armour and Swift records), Chicago History Museum, Kansas State Historical Society
records_generated.research_tip: Many packinghouse records were destroyed when plants closed. Start with the Wisconsin Historical Society, which acquired the largest collection of industry records.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Membership rolls, grievance files, seniority lists, local union meeting minutes
records_generated.where_to_find: Wisconsin Historical Society (UPWA records), State Historical Society of Iowa
records_generated.research_tip: Union grievance files can reveal specific working conditions and individual stories not found in any other record type

records_generated.what_it_shows: Name, home address, employer, and occupation for urban workers
records_generated.where_to_find: Public libraries in Chicago, Kansas City, Omaha, and other packinghouse cities; Ancestry.com has digitized many
records_generated.research_tip: City directories were published annually, allowing year-by-year tracking of when a family arrived and where they lived relative to the packinghouse

census_clues.what_to_look_for: A family from the rural South or Eastern Europe appearing in Chicago, Kansas City, or Omaha between 1910-1950 with the head of household listed as a laborer at a slaughterhouse or packing plant. Look at neighbors on the same census page — if several are also packing workers, the neighborhood is almost certainly a packinghouse district.
census_clues.red_flags: If a Southern-born Black worker appears in a packinghouse city in 1917-1918, they likely arrived during the first wave of the Great Migration, recruited by labor agents to replace workers lost to World War I military service.

cultural_context: The meatpacking industry was one of the great equalizers of American industrial life — and also one of its most brutal workplaces. Successive waves of immigrants (Germans and Irish in the 1870s-1880s, Poles and Lithuanians in the 1890s-1910s, Black Southerners in the 1910s-1940s, and Mexicans from the 1920s onward) entered at the bottom of the packinghouse hierarchy. The work was dangerous and degrading, but it paid cash wages and offered a foothold in the urban economy.

counter_narratives.claim: Meatpacking was not only immigrant labor
counter_narratives.context: By the 1940s-1950s, the UPWA had organized a remarkably integrated workforce. Black and white workers served together as union stewards and officers. The UPWA was one of the most racially progressive unions in American history, actively fighting workplace discrimination years before the Civil Rights Act.

counter_narratives.claim: Not everyone worked on the killing floor
counter_narratives.context: Packing plants employed thousands of workers in departments beyond slaughter: canning, lard rendering, fertilizer production, hide processing, cooperage (barrel-making), and office work. Women dominated the trimming and sausage departments. The range of jobs was far broader than popular imagination suggests.

source_references.author: Halpern, Rick
source_references.title: Down on the Killing Floor: Black and White Workers in Chicago's Packinghouses, 1904-54
source_references.notes: Definitive study of race relations and labor organizing in Chicago's meatpacking industry

source_references.author: Horowitz, Roger
source_references.title: Negro and White, Unite and Fight! A Social History of Industrial Unionism in Meatpacking, 1930-90
source_references.notes: Chronicles the UPWA and its pioneering role in interracial unionism

source_references.author: Barrett, James R.
source_references.title: Work and Community in the Jungle: Chicago's Packinghouse Workers, 1894-1922
source_references.notes: Examines the immigrant experience in early 20th-century packinghouses and the neighborhoods that surrounded them
